content.problemStatement:
Tsetso likes to play with arrays.

An Array A[1,2,…,N]A[1,2,\ldots,N]A[1,2,…,N] is said to be a Tsetso array if A[1]⋅A[N]=max(A1,A2,…,AN−1,AN)A[1] \cdot A[N] = max(A_1,A_2,\ldots,A_{N-1},A_N)A[1]⋅A[N]=max(A1​,A2​,…,AN−1​,AN​).

Bedo, his teammate, likes to solve problems so much, so Tsetso gave him an array AAA of NNN distinct\bf{distinct}distinct integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and QQQ queries of the form [L,R][L, R][L,R] and asked him to count the number of Tsetso subarrays in the given range.

A subarray[L,R][L,R][L,R] is to be a Tsetso subarray if A[L]⋅A[R]=max(AL,AL+1,....,AR−1,AR)A[L] \cdot A[R] = max(A_L,A_{L+1},....,A_{R-1},A_R)A[L]⋅A[R]=max(AL​,AL+1​,....,AR−1​,AR​).

Bedo spent a lot of time trying to solve it, but he couldn't, can you?

Note\bf{Note}Note: A sequence aaa is a subarray of a sequence bbb if aaa can be obtained from bbb by deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) elements from the beginning and several (possibly, zero or all) elements from the end.


Input Format

First-line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of input contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ, the number of elements in the array, and the number of queries.
The second line contains NNN distinct\bf{distinct}distinct space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
Then QQQ lines follow each line containing two space-separated integers L,RL, RL,R.


Output Format
For each query, output in a single line the number of Tsetso subarrays in the given range [L,R][L, R][L,R].
constraints: 2 ≤ N ≤ 100000 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 100000)
1 ≤ Q ≤ 500000 (sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 500000)
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q, the size of the array and the number of queries.
- The second line contains N distinct integers A1 … AN.
- Each of the next Q lines contains two integers L and R (1‑based), describing a query.
All queries are independent.
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single line containing the number of subarrays [i, j] such that L ≤ i ≤ j ≤ R and A[i]·A[j] equals the maximum element of the subarray.

solution.approach:
For a subarray [i, j] let M be its maximum element. The condition A[i]·A[j] = M can hold only in two situations:
1. One of the ends equals 1. Then the product equals the other end, which must be the maximum of the whole interval.
2. Both ends are >1. Then the product is larger than each end, therefore the product must be an interior element and it is automatically the maximum of the interval.
Hence every valid subarray has a unique element k (the position of the maximum) such that A[i]·A[j] = A[k] and k lies inside the interval. If we know for each position k the nearest greater element to the left (Lk) and to the right (Rk), any subarray that contains k and does not contain a larger element must satisfy Lk < i ≤ k ≤ j < Rk.
Therefore we can enumerate all valid subarrays by iterating over every position k (treated as the maximum) and looking for pairs (i, j) whose values multiply to A[k].
Because the array values are distinct, we only need to consider divisor pairs of A[k] that also appear in the array. For each k we:
- Factorise A[k] using Pollard‑Rho (fast for 64‑bit numbers).
- Generate all divisors of A[k].
- For each divisor d that exists in the array, let e = A[k]/d. If e also exists, obtain their positions id and jd.
- Order i = min(id, jd), j = max(id, jd). If i ≤ k ≤ j and i > Lk and j < Rk, the pair (i, j) is a valid subarray.
- If A[k] == 1 we also add the length‑1 subarray (k, k).
All found pairs (i, j) are stored.
Now each query asks for the number of stored pairs completely inside [L, R]. This is a classic offline 2‑D counting problem. We sort the queries by their right endpoint R. While scanning R from 1 to N we insert into a Fenwick tree every pair whose right endpoint equals the current position, adding +1 at its left endpoint. For a query (L, R) the answer is the sum of the Fenwick tree over indices [L, R] at the moment when the scan reaches R.
Complexities:
- Factorisation of one 64‑bit number with Pollard‑Rho is O(n^{1/4}) in practice and very fast for 10^5 numbers.
- The total number of divisor pairs that actually appear in the array is small; in the worst case it is O(N·τmax) where τmax (max number of divisors) ≤ 1030 for numbers ≤10^18, well within limits.
- Building Lk and Rk with a monotonic stack is O(N).
- Offline answering with a Fenwick tree is O((N + Q) log N).
Overall time per test case is O(N·log Ai + (N+Q)·log N) and memory O(N).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·log Ai + (N+Q)·log N) per test case (Ai factorisation, stack, Fenwick operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for arrays, stacks, Fenwick tree and the list of valid subarrays.
